item.title: Splunk CVE-2026-20253 Exploitation, Immobiliare npm Compromise, and Fake OpenAI Workspace Invites
item.content:encoded:
Critical Unauthenticated Remote Code Execution in Splunk Enterprise (CVE-2026-20253) — Must Read

Splunk Enterprise has a critical unauthenticated RCE flaw, CVE-2026-20253, that is being actively exploited and can let attackers abuse exposed PostgreSQL recovery endpoints through Splunk Web to write files and run code as the Splunk service account. Organizations should urgently upgrade affected 10.2.x and 10.0.x versions, restrict Splunk Web exposure, or temporarily disable the PostgreSQL sidecar service where safe.

  • Splunk disclosed CVE-2026-20253 on June 10, 2026 as a critical unauthenticated remote code execution vulnerability in Splunk Enterprise with a CVSS score of 9.8.
  • The vulnerability affects Splunk Enterprise 10.2.x prior to 10.2.4 and 10.0.x prior to 10.0.7, while Splunk Enterprise 9.4 and earlier and Splunk Cloud Platform are not affected.
  • Splunk updated its advisory on June 18, 2026 to warn of active exploitation, and CISA added CVE-2026-20253 to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog the same day.
  • The flaw allows an unauthenticated attacker to reach PostgreSQL sidecar recovery endpoints through Splunk Web, create or write attacker-controlled files, and ultimately execute code as the Splunk service account.
  • Recommended actions include upgrading to a fixed release, temporarily disabling the PostgreSQL sidecar service when compatible with the instance’s features, and restricting network access to the Splunk Web management interface.

Chinese-Speaking APT Deploys New TinyRCT Backdoor in Southeast Asia Campaign — Must Read

A Chinese-speaking APT tracked as CL-STA-1062 is targeting Southeast Asian government and critical infrastructure organizations with a new custom .NET backdoor called TinyRCT. The group combines web shells and open-source tools like SoftEther VPN, Mimikatz, and VNT with TinyRCT to conduct reconnaissance, lateral movement, command execution, file theft, and screenshot capture.

  • Palo Alto Networks Unit 42 linked a Chinese-speaking APT actor tracked as CL-STA-1062 to attacks on government entities and critical infrastructure in Southeast Asia.
  • Unit 42 said CL-STA-1062 shares overlaps with UAT-7237 and has targeted strategic sectors in East Asia since March 2022.
  • The group uses a hybrid toolkit that includes open-source tools such as SoftEther VPN, Mimikatz, Yuze, and VNT, as well as a new custom backdoor called TinyRCT.
  • TinyRCT can run arbitrary commands, enumerate and exfiltrate files, capture the device screen, upload files, support remote control, and delete itself from the compromised host.
  • In one September 2025 campaign, the threat actor infiltrated a Southeast Asian government entity, deployed a web shell to exfiltrate data from an MS SQL server, and conducted network reconnaissance against another government entity in the same country.

Multiple @immobiliarelabs Backstage Plugins Compromised on npm — Must Read

Multiple Immobiliare Labs Backstage npm plugins were compromised with malicious patch releases that execute during npm install via a binding.gyp node-gyp hook. The payload steals a wide range of developer, cloud, CI/CD, and registry credentials and attempts persistence through AI coding assistant configurations.

  • On June 26, 2026, multiple versions of four Immobiliare Labs Backstage npm packages were found to contain a malicious payload that executes during npm install via a binding.gyp node-gyp hook.
  • The affected packages are @immobiliarelabs/backstage-plugin-gitlab, @immobiliarelabs/backstage-plugin-gitlab-backend, @immobiliarelabs/backstage-plugin-ldap-auth, and @immobiliarelabs/backstage-plugin-ldap-auth-backend.
  • Each compromised version includes a 5 MB index.js file and a binding.gyp file that were absent from prior releases, and all compromised versions were published within a 30-second window on June 26, 2026.
  • The payload targets credentials from sources including GitHub Actions, cloud provider services, Kubernetes, Vault, package registries, password managers, and SSH keys.
  • The payload includes an infectHost function that modifies configuration files for several AI coding assistants, including Claude Code, GitHub Copilot, Cursor, VS Code, Aider, Amazon Kiro, Sourcegraph Cody, Google Gemini, and OpenAI Codex.

Cybersecurity firms targeted by fraudulent OpenAI organization invites — Should Read

Attackers are creating fake OpenAI/ChatGPT organizations that impersonate real companies and sending legitimate-looking OpenAI invites to employees. The goal appears to be tricking staff into using the attacker-controlled workspace and exposing sensitive company data in prompts, chats, or projects.

  • Threat actors are creating OpenAI tenants that impersonate legitimate companies and inviting employees to join them.
  • Push Security discovered the campaign after multiple employees received legitimate OpenAI invitations to an organization named “Push Security Inc.” that had been created by an attacker using Gmail addresses.
  • The invitation emails came from OpenAI’s legitimate notification address, passed email authentication checks, and looked like normal ChatGPT workspace invitations.
  • After accepting an invitation, a Push Security researcher found a fraudulent organization containing an attacker-controlled Gmail account posing as the company’s CEO and a Visa credit card attached to billing.
  • Push Security believes the attackers aim to trick employees into using the fake ChatGPT workspace so they submit sensitive information such as source code, internal documents, customer data, security research, or strategic plans.

Your First GRC Agent: A Red Teamer's Walkthrough — Optional

The article argues that agentic AI can make GRC more continuous and evidence-driven by monitoring controls, detecting drift, and creating findings automatically while preserving human approval for high-impact decisions. Its main advice is to start with low-risk, repetitive GRC tasks and require strong execution logs, least privilege, and human sign-off to build trust.

  • The article says agentic GRC systems differ from traditional automation because they can act when conditions are met, use current program context, and execute multiple steps such as analyzing, deciding, and acting in sequence.
  • The author argues that AI in GRC should support rather than replace human judgment, with models used for reasoning, summarization, and orchestration while humans define controls, thresholds, and policy decisions.
  • The article describes an example agent in Anecdotes Agent Studio that checks whether MFA evidence for ISO 27001:2022 control A.8.5 is older than 24 hours, queries an identity provider such as Okta or Entra ID, compares the policy against a baseline, and opens a finding if a group is out of enforcement.
  • The article says each agent run should produce an execution log showing the trigger, data read, comparison performed, decision reached, action taken, and evidence touched so that decisions can be reconstructed and reviewed.
  • The author recommends starting with high-toil, low-judgment tasks such as evidence gap detection, extracting findings from audit reports, or generating analysis rules before expanding agentic GRC use to higher-stakes areas.

Rolldown Pulls Rust React Compiler Integration After Binary Size Increase — Optional

Rolldown and Vite removed a Rust-based React Compiler integration because it increased the default binary size by about 5MB, which maintainers said was too much for a framework-agnostic tool used by many non-React projects. The team is exploring smaller and more opt-in approaches that could still bring React users native compiler speedups without making all users carry the cost.

  • Rolldown and Vite withdrew a Rust-based React Compiler integration after maintainers decided the increase from 28.7MB to 33.8MB, or 17%, was too costly for a feature shipped to all users by default.
  • The integration originated from Oxc work intended to let React apps avoid Babel pipelines when they only needed Babel for React Compiler, and it was later wired into Rolldown behind a new transform.reactCompiler option before the Rolldown PR was closed on June 9.
  • Critics argued that React-specific compiler support should not add weight to Vite or Rolldown core because Vite is framework-agnostic and used across many frameworks.
  • Evan You said Vite cannot give special treatment to React without raising the same question for Vue, Svelte, Solid, Astro, and other frameworks, especially given Vite’s 12 million weekly downloads.
  • Boshen is experimenting with a smaller implementation that he said shows up to a 2x performance improvement with only a 1.4MB binary size increase, but the code still needs to be made maintainable and upstreamed.

item.title: Turla StockStay, Windchill RCE, Linux pedit COW/DirtyClone, and Miasma Supply Chain Attacks
Turla STOCKSTAY Targets Ukraine as Cellebrite Russia Use and Packagist Malware Blocking Emerge
